title: Pierre Robin Sequence: Airway Management
objectives: Understand the sequence of events in Pierre Robin
Apply management algorithm from conservative to surgical

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**The Sequence:**

1. **Micrognathia** (primary defect)
   ↓
2. **Glossoptosis** (tongue falls back)
   ↓
3. **Airway Obstruction**
   ↓
4. **U-shaped Cleft Palate** (tongue prevents palate fusion)

**Key Concept:** This is a SEQUENCE (one defect leads to others), not a syndrome

**Incidence:** 1/8,500-14,000 births

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Airway Obstruction:**
• Worse when supine
• Improves with prone positioning
• Stertor, stridor
• Retractions, desaturations
• Cyanotic episodes

**Feeding Difficulties:**
• Poor coordination
• Aspiration risk
• Failure to thrive

**Physical Exam:**
• Retrognathic mandible
• Tongue position against posterior pharynx
• Wide U-shaped cleft palate

sections.title: Airway Assessment
sections.content:
**Severity Classification:**

**Mild:**
• Obstruction only in supine position
• Relieved by prone positioning
• No desaturations

**Moderate:**
• Obstruction in multiple positions
• Intermittent desaturations
• Feeding difficulties

**Severe:**
• Obstruction despite positioning
• Frequent/prolonged desaturations
• Unable to feed orally
• May require intubation

**Workup:**
• Sleep study (polysomnography)
• Flexible laryngoscopy
• Swallow study if feeding issues

sections.title: Management - Conservative
sections.content:
**Positioning:**
• Prone or lateral positioning
• Effective in 50-70% of cases
• Gravity helps tongue fall forward

**Nasopharyngeal Airway:**
• Bypasses tongue obstruction
• Bridge to definitive treatment
• Requires monitoring

**Feeding Support:**
• Special nipples/bottles
• NG tube if inadequate oral intake
• Upright positioning during feeds
• May need G-tube

**Non-invasive Ventilation:**
• CPAP for moderate cases
• Stents airway open

sections.title: Management - Surgical
sections.content:
**Indications for Surgery:**
• Failed conservative management
• Severe obstruction
• Failure to thrive
• Recurrent aspiration

**Surgical Options:**

**Tongue-Lip Adhesion:**
• Temporary procedure
• Sutures tongue to lip
• Prevents glossoptosis

**Mandibular Distraction Osteogenesis:**
• Gradually lengthens mandible
• Addresses underlying micrognathia
• 1mm/day advancement
• Most definitive treatment

**Tracheostomy:**
• Last resort for severe cases
• Bypasses upper airway entirely
• Significant morbidity

sections.title: Associated Syndromes
sections.content:
**Isolated PRS:** 40-50%

**Syndromic PRS:** 50-60%

**Stickler Syndrome (most common):**
• Myopia, retinal detachment
• Joint hypermobility
• Collagen disorder

**Velocardiofacial/22q11:**
• Cardiac defects
• Immune deficiency

**Treacher Collins:**
• Mandibulofacial dysostosis

**Fetal Alcohol Syndrome**

**Evaluation:**
• Genetics consultation
• Echocardiogram
• Ophthalmology exam
• Hearing evaluation

keyTakeaways: Pierre Robin is a SEQUENCE, not a syndrome
Micrognathia is the primary defect leading to other features
50-60% of cases are associated with syndromes (especially Stickler)
Mandibular distraction addresses the underlying cause

reviewQuestions.question: A newborn with micrognathia and cleft palate has oxygen desaturations while supine that resolve when placed prone. Polysomnography shows AHI of 8. What is the most appropriate initial management?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Mild-moderate Pierre Robin sequence often responds to conservative positioning. Prone positioning allows gravity to pull the tongue forward, relieving obstruction. Surgery is reserved for failed conservative management.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected pierre robin?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in pierre robin. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of pierre robin. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing pierre robin, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
